title: NDIS fraudsters heading to prison in taskforce blitz
full_text:
A total of 17 NDIS fraudsters have been sentenced to more than 60 years in jail collectively, with new data revealing two-thirds of NDIS-related criminal charges have resulted in a conviction.
Of the 26 NDIS-related convictions, two offenders were hit with six-year jail sentences.
A further seven matters are expected to go to sentencing in the coming months, while 15 fraud cases are before the courts and evidence is being assessed by the Commonwealth Director of Public Prosecutions for 24 individuals.
In the financial year to May 31, the Fraud Fusion Taskforce has assessed more than 88,000 tips, and executed 50 search warrants relating to more than $118m in NDIS claims.
NDIS Minister Jenny McAllister heralded the work done by the 25-member Fraud Fusion Taskforce and said the group was “knocking on more doors, uncovering more fraud and putting more criminals before the courts”.
“If you seek to drain the plans of people with disability, then we are coming for you,” she said.
“There is nothing lower than trying to steal money from a disabled person. “Criminals who try to rip off NDIS participants will be thrown in jail.” It comes as Labor is attempting to legislate generational reforms to the disability schemes.
The government aims to curtail its growth from about 10 per cent this financial year to 2 per cent by 2030, and 5 to 6 per cent in the years after.
While the Coalition has promised support to pass the reforms, Labor agreed to delay passage of the bills and extend an inquiry into the reforms, costing millions in potential savings, in exchange for support from the Greens to waive through its controversial tax reforms. The new laws will aim to recover up to $4.6bn lost to fraud each year through mandatory provider registration and new civil penalties, and give the NDIA more powers to investigate fraudulent claims.
